description: 30-slide DALP deck focused on configurable token design and precious metals tokenization

slides.title: DALP for Tokenized Metals
slides.subtitle: Institutional lifecycle control for configurable digital assets

slides.title: Executive summary
slides.body:
• Tokenization is easy to demo but difficult to operate once identity, compliance, custody coordination, settlement, and servicing matter together.
• DALP is SettleMint’s digital asset lifecycle platform for designing, launching, and operating tokenized assets on EVM-compatible networks.
• DALP stands out through configurable asset design: governed templates, policy modules, identity-linked controls, role-based approvals, and lifecycle actions on one platform.
• Precious metals are a strong fit because investors care about provenance, vault data, fractional access, transfer rules, and redemption discipline as much as the token itself.

slides.title: Why tokenization gets hard
slides.subtitle: Production complexity starts after the mint

slides.title: Easy to demo, hard to run
slides.body:
• A simple mint proves a token can exist; it does not prove the operating model can survive legal review, onboarding, settlement, and day-two servicing.
• Production programs need product, compliance, treasury, operations, and engineering teams working from the same asset record instead of separate spreadsheets and side processes.
• Manual controls create exception queues, reconciliation risk, slower launches, and weak evidence for audit or partner review.
• The real challenge is not creating a token contract. It is governing the full lifecycle without rebuilding the stack for every new asset.

slides.title: What institutions must control
slides.item1:
Investor eligibility
Institutions must control who may subscribe, hold, and transfer, not just which wallet receives a token.
slides.item2:
Transfer conditions
Jurisdiction, supply, time-lock, and role-based restrictions must be enforced before execution.
slides.item3:
Servicing events
Issuance is only the start; live assets need payouts, freezes, redemptions, reporting, and exception handling.
slides.item4:
Operational evidence
Approvals, logs, monitoring, and reconciliation outputs must support governance and external review.

slides.title: What DALP is
slides.subtitle: One platform for the digital asset lifecycle

slides.title: DALP in one sentence
slides.body:
• DALP is SettleMint’s Digital Asset Lifecycle Platform for designing, launching, and operating tokenized assets with governance, identity, compliance, settlement coordination, and servicing on one control plane.
• It is built for regulated institutions that need repeatability, auditability, and production reliability rather than isolated proofs of concept.
• The platform supports seven pre-built asset classes: bonds, equity, funds, deposits, stablecoins, real estate, and precious metals.
• DALP extends beyond fixed templates through a configurable asset model, while staying grounded in approved EVM-compatible deployment language.

slides.title: DALP platform pillars
slides.col1:
Governed design
• Use asset classes and reusable templates.
• Keep metadata and terms explicit.
• Review launch readiness before deployment.
• Reuse approved patterns across future programs.
slides.col2:
Policy execution
• Link identity to permissions.
• Enforce rules before transfer.
• Separate maker and checker actions.
slides.col3:
Live operations
• Run servicing on-platform.
• Integrate custody and core systems.
• Monitor with audit-ready evidence.

slides.title: Supported asset model
slides.body: DALP supports multiple regulated asset classes on the same operating model. The platform UI demonstrates that precious metals are not treated as a special one-off demo; they sit alongside bonds, equity, funds, deposits, stablecoins, and real estate inside the same lifecycle environment. That matters because institutions want reuse across products, not separate stacks by asset class.

slides.title: Lifecycle on one platform
slides.body: DALP is designed around the full digital asset lifecycle. Asset design, onboarding, issuance, transfer, settlement coordination, servicing, redemption, and reporting operate as connected stages rather than separate tools. That is the core difference between a demo environment and production infrastructure.

slides.title: Configurable token design
slides.subtitle: How DALP turns controls into product configuration

slides.title: Why configurable tokens matter
slides.body:
• Institutions rarely launch one generic token repeatedly; they launch instruments with different terms, permissions, distribution rules, and servicing needs.
• DALP treats the asset as a governed configuration problem: choose an asset class, define metadata, attach policy modules, set permissions, and connect servicing actions.
• That reduces custom smart-contract work while making product, legal, operations, and engineering teams collaborate on one explicit design.
• The benefit is not only faster launch. It is better control over what the token is allowed to do in production.

slides.title: Token design building blocks
slides.item1:
Asset class
Start from a supported product type with governed defaults.
slides.item2:
Metadata schema
Capture the fields that define the real instrument.
slides.item3:
Supply rules
Control issuance amounts, caps, and release logic.
slides.item4:
Identity gates
Require verified claims before distribution or transfer.
slides.item5:
Permissions
Assign approval, custody, and operational roles clearly.
slides.item6:
Lifecycle actions
Enable mint, burn, freeze, pause, redeem, and related flows.

slides.title: Asset designer and templates
slides.body: The DALP asset designer gives product and operations teams a structured path from instrument definition to review and deployment. Instead of free-form contract changes, teams work through explicit configuration steps for asset details, pricing, compliance modules, permissions, and launch readiness. That makes token design repeatable and reviewable.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/03 - Asset Designer/Asset Designer - Step 6 - Compliance Modules.jpg

slides.title: Compliance modules in workflow
slides.body: Compliance is part of the product workflow, not a document that lives beside it. DALP policy templates expose reusable controls for identity, country restrictions, supply conditions, holding periods, and settlement-related rules. Teams can apply and review policy logic during setup instead of rebuilding controls for each launch.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/11 - Compliance and Policy Templates/Policy Templates - List.jpg

slides.title: Identity and claims
slides.body: Verified identity is central to DALP’s operating model. OnchainID and reusable claims let institutions control who may receive, hold, and move a regulated token. This supports cleaner onboarding, more consistent transfer decisions, and a distribution model based on eligibility rather than raw wallet addresses.

slides.title: Governance and lifecycle controls
slides.item1:
Role-based authority
RBAC and maker-checker controls separate configuration, approval, and execution responsibilities.
slides.item2:
Lifecycle actions
Mint, burn, freeze, pause, redeem, and related controls stay connected to policy and permissions.
slides.item3:
Emergency response
Operational controls support intervention when an exception, breach, or servicing issue appears.
slides.item4:
Reusable governance
The same control model can be applied across products instead of reinventing procedures per asset.

slides.title: Transfer decision flow
slides.body: This flow captures DALP’s compliance posture around token movement. The platform resolves the token policy, checks identity and claims, evaluates transfer conditions, and only then allows execution or blocks the action. For regulated assets, the key is ex-ante control with an evidence trail after the decision.

slides.title: Precious metals tokenization
slides.subtitle: A strong use case for configurable digital assets

slides.title: Why metals are strong RWAs
slides.body:
• Precious metals already have widely understood economic properties: scarcity, reserve value, standard custody practices, and global investor demand.
• Tokenization can improve distribution, fractional access, transferability, and operational transparency without changing the underlying asset thesis.
• The production challenge is provenance and control: investors need confidence in vault location, custodian data, eligibility rules, and redemption handling.
• That is exactly where configurable token design matters more than a simple commodity token demo.

slides.title: Metals as a governed class
slides.body: DALP includes precious metals as a supported asset class rather than forcing teams into a generic contract. The platform UI shows multiple metal programs across gold, silver, platinum, and palladium, with vault-linked naming and fractional token precision. This grounds the story in real-world custody structure instead of abstract token theory.

slides.title: Physical metadata made digital
slides.body: For metals, the token must carry more than supply and symbol. DALP’s precious-metal detail view shows asset-specific metadata such as metal type, storage location, and custodian. That is important because a precious metal token is only credible when the digital record stays linked to physical custody facts and servicing responsibility.

slides.title: Metals issuance and servicing
slides.body: A precious-metals program needs a controlled sequence from product design through distribution and redemption. DALP helps connect vault metadata, investor identity, issuance rules, transfer checks, monitoring, and close-out workflows inside one platform record. That is how fractional access and operational discipline can coexist.

slides.title: Why configurable tokens fit metals
slides.col1:
Investor and market side
• Fractional ownership broadens access.
• Eligibility rules protect distribution.
• Transfer checks support controlled secondary movement.
• Reporting improves transparency.
slides.col2:
Asset and operator side
• Metadata links token to vault facts.
• Permissions separate issuer, ops, and custody roles.
• Freeze, redeem, and burn actions stay governed.
• Integrations keep servicing auditable.

slides.title: Metals requirements mapped to DALP
slides.body: Precious metals are a good test of whether a platform is truly configurable. The token must reflect custody facts, investor controls, lifecycle events, and operational evidence at the same time.

slides.title: Architecture, ops, and custody
slides.col1:
Architecture
• DALP sits between core systems and EVM networks.
• APIs, events, and identity stay on one control plane.
• Deployment can be public or permissioned EVM.
slides.col2:
Operations and compliance
• Monitoring, logs, and approvals support live programs.
• Policies are enforced before execution.
• Auditability spans requests and outcomes.
slides.col3:
Custody and settlement
• DALP is not the custodian.
• Bring-your-own-custodian supports Fireblocks and DFNS.
• Settlement and servicing integrate into the workflow.

slides.title: Why SettleMint wins
slides.item1:
Platform, not point solution
DALP covers design, identity, compliance, settlement coordination, servicing, and reporting in one operating model.
slides.item2:
Configurable by design
Templates, policy modules, permissions, and lifecycle controls fit institutional tokenization better than fixed commodity-token tooling.
slides.item3:
Credible for regulated teams
RBAC, maker-checker workflows, observability, and auditability support production governance from day one.
slides.item4:
Clear deployment boundary
Approved positioning is EVM-compatible network support with integration into existing custody, risk, and core environments.

slides.title: Example implementation path
slides.col1:
1. Define the product
• Confirm metal program scope.
• Choose vault, custodian, and distribution model.
• Map policy requirements.
slides.col2:
2. Configure DALP
• Set metadata and supply rules.
• Attach identity and compliance modules.
• Connect custody and operations flows.
slides.col3:
3. Launch and scale
• Start with one governed issuance.
• Prove servicing and reporting.
• Reuse the model across new assets.
